name: Pogo-Sticking
summary: When a user clicks a search result, quickly returns to the SERP, and clicks a different result, signaling that the first result did not satisfy their query.
explanation:
Pogo-sticking is a user behavior pattern in search where someone clicks on a search result, quickly returns to the search engine results page (SERP), and then clicks on a different result. This back-and-forth behavior signals to search engines that the first result failed to satisfy the user's query, making it a potential negative quality signal.

Pogo-sticking differs from a regular bounce. A bounce simply means a user visited only one page—they might have found exactly what they needed and left satisfied. Pogo-sticking specifically involves returning to the SERP and trying another result, which more clearly indicates dissatisfaction with the clicked result.

Common causes of pogo-sticking include: misleading title tags or meta descriptions that promise content the page doesn't deliver; thin content that doesn't adequately answer the query; poor user experience (slow loading, intrusive ads, difficult navigation); content that doesn't match the searcher's intent (e.g., an advanced technical page when the user wanted a beginner guide); and outdated information that's no longer accurate.

While search engines haven't explicitly confirmed pogo-sticking as a ranking factor, it's widely believed to influence results. Google's Search Quality Raters Guidelines emphasize user satisfaction, and pogo-sticking is one of the clearest behavioral signals of dissatisfaction. Persistent pogo-sticking on a result may lead to ranking demotions.

Reducing pogo-sticking requires: accurately representing page content in title tags and meta descriptions; ensuring content thoroughly addresses the likely search intent; providing immediate value above the fold; optimizing page speed; and structuring content so users can quickly confirm the page will answer their question.
